paragraphs.body: How do we change a world not made for us

I have been my own imposter ever since I was seven years old to fit the confines of normalcy. Being neurodivergent means my brain processes things differently than others and it can be harder for me to understand certain things, I have always felt like most individuals operated on a different wavelength than I did and have desperately wanted to feel like I belonged just as I am. “A World Not Made for us” an article written by Pual Fidalgo, a forty-five-year-old white neurodivergent man acknowledges that the world we live in was not made for anyone of us but each person in this world is a traveler who walks their own path, each person has their own strengths, weaknesses, and obstacles, and to each person their own unique experiences. It is only when kindness is used in consideration of these experiences can we free ourselves and create the change we want to see. He demonstrates his point by developing a few important points. First Fidalgo refers to his experience of feeling alienated on behalf of the neurodivergent community. Second he accounts for the privilege he has while also relaying how covid 19 and his father’s passing from brain cancer gained him new perspective. Finally Fidalgo proposes that recognizing how society treats us as well as how other people have their own differing experiences is a feat in maturity.

annotations.text: I have always felt like most individuals operated on a different wavelength than I did and have desperately wanted to feel like I belonged just as I am.
annotations.comment: authentic, lovely, vulnerable statement. It's compelling.
Let's talk about the "art" of summary. What parts of this summary are necessary to "point" your reader to your essay's agenda?
annotations.context_left: but each person in this world is a traveler who walks their own path, each person has their own

paragraphs.body: I was at a seafood restaurant at the beach with my family, it seemed like the rest of night was going to be a fun time as I looked toward my mother who is boisterously laughing with my little brother Mac. She glanced at me then back at Mac and then intoxicatedly jeers “yeah well you know she’s not that smart that’s for sure! Ha ha!” the next morning she apologized for it, and most likely soon forgot it even happened, I didn’t. A fun superpower that comes with being a bit of an odd ball like me is that you get to split yourself into two halves, Real and Fake. Unfortunately I have spent most of my life developing this concealed version of myself to fit into social pressure. The real me survived brain cancer, shoots short movies, enjoys swimming, hoards sketch books, loves Nintendo, collects records, builds Lego, and goes thrifting from time to time. Fidalgo admits that the neurodivergent community often have struggles when it comes expectations of everyday situations. In my case I struggle with comprehension, mood swings, hearing and distraction. He shares his official diagnosis of autism and ADHD came extremely late, when he was 38 years old. Not to mention he also details to have been masking it for an incredible nearly 50 years which I could not possibly fathom. Masking is when someone hides their natural behavior and personality to avoid harassment and abuse. Speaking from experience, pretending to be this false version of yourself requires a lot of mental and emotional energy but it also can further imbed a further shame into being born neurodivergent in the first place. My younger brother of two years, Mac, would often call me retarded among other worse things, throughout my teenage years but Dad always filed that under the “boys will be boys” or “that just means he likes you” category. So I guess I was expected to forget about it, but I didn’t. Fidalgo elaborates on how his diagnosis led to a revelation that he was not at fault for how his brain worked. I have had select teachers who would ignore my accommodation and make my existence feel like a burden before entire groups of students, that didn’t sting nearly as bad as the many times my family didn’t use kindness in considering my unique experiences after jabs they made toward me that created gradual scars forever inscribed in my head. In all seriousness they actually care for and support me but will often seize any moment to point out or “correct” my short comings. Despite sometimes pulling the “woe is me” Schtick, I do indeed have a lot to consider in the realm of privilege. I am a bisexual white American college student who was born into the middle class with access to insurance and medical care. In many ways the things I complain about on a day-to-day basis are very trivial. Similarly Fidalgo, who is also white, American, and college educated, explains “my experience of the world is also likely to be very different from someone of a different race, sex, gender identity, or sexual orientation.” And “nor do I have any idea what it is like to be deaf, blind, or immobilized. One doesn’t need to be on any kind of neurological spectrum to feel like the world hasn’t been made with someone like them in mind.” In other words Fidalgo believes that you don’t need to fit a certain criteria to feel unbelonging in this world, everyone has their own experiences and differences. Up until now I have relayed instances in which the world seemed to reject me, although I can’t help but think of a few other people who had been disregarded far more than myself. Back in Georgia, Mac had been in a majority black friend group at his school. His best friend had suffered bullying and drug problems, I used to go to that same school, it was predominantly white and is considered to be one of the best public high schools of the state. While I don’t claim to know the whole story, I believe maybe if one of the nearly two hundred faculty members had used kindness and consideration to pay just that little bit more attention, his family possibly may not have had to feel the heartbreak of losing a child. My brother certainly never forgot his best friend, whose face is plastered on his phone wallpaper. My high school experience was pretty great, with almost all my teachers tending to my needs but I can’t say the same for one of my peers at the time. Her name was Gigi and admittedly she did have some behavioral issues, but my anger prone tyrannical sophomore lit teacher seemed to help her out through them up until he realized through the first half of the semester that she was transgender. From then on he would target her anytime she did anything he didn’t like whether it was her looking through her pencil pouch for a highlighter or taking longer to set up her notes. The thing is that you didn’t need to be in the room to know he was upset because you could hear him screaming crystal clear down to the other side of the hall. I fall guilty of taking the bystander stance, I knew my deafening teacher was in the wrong and so did the other students, but I think it is safe to say we were all too terrified of our power-hungry instructor to speak out against him. I know that she didn’t forget being tormented for 40 minutes a day-five days a week for months on end. There are countless numbers of people out there that have severe disabilities and conditions, and endless times where it is evident society is not pandering toward them. Fidalgo accounts for his experience of being afflicted with covid 19 and how it gave him an inches perspective into how his departed Dad possibly felt when battling brain cancer. He goes on to discuss that despite nearly one billion people where infected with the virus, the world does not cater to the sick as it neither does the disabled and neurodivergent. My twin is one of those countless numbers of people dealing with severe disabilities, His name is Broc, and he is on the autism spectrum. Broc has the mental age of a four-year-old and he likes watching animated shows with bright colors. Currently his favorite show is Bluey on Disney junior and honestly I would be lying if I told you that I was not a fan, so this is one of the many things that we bond over. My family often worries about him though because even being a grown man he views the world through the eyes of a toddler and so if somebody wanted to take advantage of him they easily could. In fact in elementary school a teacher had told my parents she would sue them because he had broken her wrist, this did not happen. The teacher broke her wrist trying to catch a heavy picture frame that had fallen of the classroom wall. What is most interesting though is that she had the balls to say something so outlandish in front of my mom who had the ability of playing uno reverse card onto this unsuspecting jerk wad.

paragraphs.body: Fidalgo focuses on using kindness in consideration of other people’s unique experiences as it is when we do this we mature and gain the ability to create the world we want to see. I agree that it is extremely important to try to step into someone else’s shoes. I can acknowledge that our world was not created for anyone person. But how do we make the world better when there are so many people, some of whom are in authoritarian positions, who actively refuse to consider others experiences altogether and instead ostracize them for being different?
